title: Nmap OS Detection
description: Nmap's technique for identifying the operating system of a target by analyzing TCP/IP stack behavior.
fullExplanation: Nmap OS detection (-O flag) sends a series of specially crafted TCP, UDP, and ICMP probes to the target and analyzes the responses. Different operating systems implement the TCP/IP stack differently — initial TTL values, TCP window sizes, IP ID sequences, TCP options, and ICMP response behavior all vary. Nmap compares the observed fingerprint against a database of 2600+ known OS fingerprints to identify the OS and version.

techniques.description: Analyze Initial Sequence Number patterns — different OSes use different ISN generation algorithms
How IP ID field increments — sequential, random, or zero (Linux)
Frequency and behavior of TCP timestamp option
Initial window size varies by OS (Linux: 29200, Windows: 8192, macOS: 65535)
Order and values of TCP options in SYN packets
How OS responds to various ICMP types and codes

commonFingerprints.notes: Sequential IP ID, no timestamps by default

keyFacts: Requires root/admin — needs to send raw packets
Needs at least one open and one closed port for accurate detection
Accuracy: ~95% for common OSes, lower for embedded/custom stacks
Firewall/IDS can detect OS detection probes — unusual packet combinations
nmap -O --osscan-guess: show best guess even if confidence is low
Passive OS fingerprinting: p0f analyzes traffic without sending probes
